incurable disease
the body given over in judgment

schools.summary: The king carried into captivity — 'the LORD shall bring thee, and thy king... unto a nation which neither thou nor thy fathers have known; and there shalt thou serve other gods, wood and stone'; fulfilled in Jehoiachin and Zedekiah, kings of Judah, carried captive to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kgs 24:15, 25:7, Gill); the calamity so complete that even the king could not escape, much less the common people, and so irrecoverable since he who should have been their defence was himself a captive (Wesley); Moses showing his foreknowledge that Israel would have a king (Deut 17:14), a thing no later writer could conceal or invent (Ellicott); the bitterest part, forced in exile to serve the idols of their captors, the people who would not serve the living God serving dead wood and stone; the fulfilment teaching the Jews, too late, it was no empty threat but the sure word of God (Calvin).

themes: thy king which thou shalt set over thee
unto a nation not known
serve other gods, wood and stone
king and people into captivity

schools.summary: Israel made a byword among the nations — 'thou shalt become an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword, among all nations whither the LORD shall lead thee'; an astonishment to the nations that heard of their overthrow and saw their misery, a proverb and byword, their very name a token of disaster and a jest (Gill); the exact contrary of the blessing of Deut 28:10, that the peoples should see them called by the name of the LORD and fear them, instead the byword of the peoples, verified in the first captivity as Solomon foretold of the ruined temple and nation (1 Kgs 9:7, Ellicott); the very climax of their miseries, so far from finding consolation from men that on every side their misery met with taunts and insults, nothing more bitterly wounding the wretched than to be mocked in affliction (Calvin); the name of the Jew through the ages of dispersion a byword and reproach, their fall a proverb.

themes: an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword
among all nations
the contrary of the blessing
scorn added to ruin

schools.summary: The curse of fruitless labour — 'thou shalt carry much seed out into the field, and shalt gather but little in; for the locust shall consume it'; referring to the people while yet in their own land, before the captivity, sowing plentifully in expectation of a full harvest but reaping only a little (Gill); the harvest scanty notwithstanding the abundant sowing, because the locust would devour the crop, the increase that should have come from their toil eaten up by the destroyer before they could gather it (Calvin); to labour and sow and then see the fruit devoured a peculiar torment — the disappointment of hope, the waste of toil, the watching of one's substance perish; the very blessing of the land, its fruitfulness, turned to a curse, what God gives in mercy he withholds by such plagues in judgment; the increase of the field in God's hand, and he can blast the labour of the disobedient.

themes: much seed out, but little in
the locust shall consume it
fruitless labour
the increase blasted

schools.summary: The curse on the vineyard — 'thou shalt plant vineyards, and dress them, but shalt neither drink of the wine, nor gather the grapes; for the worms shall eat them'; planting the vines and pruning them in expectation of much fruit, bestowing all the care and toil a vineyard requires, yet neither gathering the grapes nor drinking the wine (Gill, Keil); the worm or grub devouring the fruit and the vine, so that all their dressing should come to nothing, the labour of the husbandman profiting no one; the curse of frustrated labour and disappointed hope — to do all the work and lose all the reward, to plant and not to drink, a sore vexation and a token of God's displeasure; in mercy God gave his people to plant vineyards and eat the fruit (Deut 6:11), but in judgment to plant and not to gather, the same vine that should have gladdened them made a grief; God turning the gladdest gift to bitterness when his people forsake him.

themes: plant vineyards, and dress them
neither drink the wine nor gather the grapes
the worms shall eat them
care without fruit

schools.summary: The curse on the olive — 'thou shalt have olive trees throughout all thy coasts, but thou shalt not anoint thyself with the oil; for thine olive shall cast his fruit'; the land of Canaan a land of olive oil, abounding in olive trees in every part, yet the people not to have the use of the oil though the trees stood in all their borders (Gill); the olive dropping its fruit before it ripened, the unripe olives falling and perishing, so that no oil could be pressed, the tree standing but yielding nothing for use; the appearance of plenty — the field sown, the vineyard planted, the olive trees standing — and yet the substance of want, the trees mocking them with a fruitfulness that came to nothing; the anointing oil a token of gladness, health, and refreshment, the want of it a mark of mourning and poverty; God giving the means of plenty yet withholding its enjoyment, blasting the increase at the last.

themes: olive trees throughout thy coasts
not anoint thyself with the oil
thine olive shall cast his fruit
plenty in show, want in substance

schools.summary: The children lost to captivity — 'thou shalt beget sons and daughters, but thou shalt not enjoy them; for they shall go into captivity'; 'they shall not be thine,' the children taken from the parents and given to others, the joy and comfort of offspring denied them (Gill); the parents begetting sons and daughters but not keeping them, because they would be carried away into captivity by the enemy, the children torn from their arms and led into a strange land (Keil); of all losses the loss of one's children among the most grievous, to bear and rear them and then see them carried off into slavery a sorrow that pierces the heart, the curse striking not only at their goods but at their dearest affections; children, the blessing and heritage of the LORD (Ps 127:3), become a grief, begotten only to be lost; the disobedient stripped of all — field, vine, olive, and their own offspring.

themes: beget sons and daughters
but thou shalt not enjoy them
they shall go into captivity
the dearest things taken

schools.summary: The locust devouring all — 'all thy trees and fruit of thy land shall the locust consume'; the locust a creature that consumes not only the grass, herbs, and corn of the field but the green trees also, stripping them bare, nothing of the land's increase escaping it (Gill); the word tselatsal, from a root 'to buzz,' a vivid epithet for the locust, naming it by the whirring of its wings, the very sound of the swarm a token of coming ruin (Keil); field, vine, olive, and tree, all the produce of the land given over to the locust, the destroyer leaving nothing, the whole increase swallowed; against the locust coming in its clouds no labour or care availing, the husbandman watching his year's work devoured in a day; the smallest of creatures at God's command undoing the labour of a nation, the disobedient at the mercy of the meanest instruments of judgment (Joel 1:4).

themes: all thy trees and fruit
the locust shall consume
the destroyer takes all
the meanest instrument of judgment

schools.summary: The reversal of rank between native and stranger — 'the stranger that is within thee shall get up above thee very high; and thou shalt come down very low'; the stranger rising in wealth, power, authority, and honour, the stranger within their gates who formerly served them and were glad of the crumbs from their table now lifted above them (Gill, Wesley); Israel utterly impoverished, sinking lower and lower, while the stranger rose higher, the order of things wholly overturned (Keil); a no doubtful mark of God's wrath, that the sojourners, who dwelt in Canaan only by sufferance, should become its masters, as the debtor falls under the power of his creditor (Calvin); the chosen people, set high among the nations in obedience, in their fall made lowest of all, beneath the very strangers in their gates; God had promised in obedience to set them on high above all nations (Deut 28:1), in disobedience the stranger set above them; pride and rebellion repaid with abasement.

themes: the stranger shall get up above thee
thou shalt come down very low
the order reversed
the chosen made lowest

schools.summary: The stranger become the head — 'he shall lend to thee, and thou shalt not lend to him: he shall be the head, and thou shalt be the tail'; the stranger in a position to lend to the Jew, while the Jew, his circumstances so reduced, not able to lend, the one rich and the other poor, the one a creditor and the other a debtor (Gill); the stranger holding the chief place, ruling and leading, the Israelite the lowest, following and serving, the head and the tail naming the highest and meanest; the exact opposite of the blessing promised in obedience, that Israel should lend to many nations and not borrow, and be the head not the tail (Deut 28:12-13), the curse turning the very words of the blessing upside down (Keil); the people who in obedience should have led the nations and lent to them, in disobedience led and borrowing, beneath the stranger in their own land; to refuse God's service being to fall under the service of men.

themes: he shall lend to thee
he shall be the head, thou the tail
the blessing reversed
under the service of men

schools.summary: The curses pursuing to destruction — 'all these curses shall come upon thee, and shall pursue thee, and overtake thee, till thou be destroyed; because thou hearkenedst not unto the voice of the LORD thy God'; how deep the treasures of that wrath are, the curses personified as hunters that chase the fugitive, run him down, and overtake him, leaving no escape (Henry); 'destroyed' meaning not utterly exterminated but crushed, broken, ground down (2 Kgs 13:7), the nation shattered though a remnant remain (Ellicott); Moses taking away all hope of alleviation, the scourges not ceasing until the people repented, following them without intermission so long as their disobedience lasted (Calvin); the cause — not chance nor misfortune but the just recompense of refusing to obey God; hitherto Moses foretelling the captivity in Babylon, in the following the later and more dreadful dispersion under the Romans (Wesley).

themes: all these curses shall pursue thee
overtake thee, till thou be destroyed
because thou hearkenedst not
the wrath unwearied

schools.summary: The curses a lasting sign and wonder — 'they shall be upon thee for a sign and for a wonder, and upon thy seed for ever'; the curses resting and remaining upon them, visible and continuing, so plainly that all who saw should observe and be amazed, their very condition a sign to the nations (Gill); a wonder amazing in the history of the world — that a people so closely incorporated, so distinct a nation, should be so universally dispersed among all peoples, and yet that a people scattered into all the nations should not, as every other scattered people, mingle and be lost, but remain through all the ages a separate and recognizable people, the standing monument of this prophecy (Wesley); the preservation of the Jews in their dispersion, distinct and unabsorbed, itself a continuing sign and wonder, an open proof of God's word, both in the curse fulfilled and in the people preserved for the future mercy (Rom 11:25); the sign upon their seed, generation after generation.

themes: for a sign and for a wonder
upon thy seed for ever
scattered yet not lost
a standing monument of prophecy

schools.summary: The sin of unthankful service — 'because thou servedst not the LORD thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart, for the abundance of all things'; the failure both in not attending God's worship and keeping his commandments, and in not doing so with a willing and joyful heart, the service God requires not grudging or cold but cheerful and glad (Gill); 'for the abundance of all things' — failing to serve God joyfully in the very time when he had loaded them with all blessings, their ingratitude the deeper because their mercies were the greater, abundance of all things from God's hand and yet not serving him gladly (Keil); God taking account not only whether we serve him but how, whether with joy and thankfulness or reluctance and complaint, to enjoy his abundant gifts without serving him gladly a great sin, the basest ingratitude; because they would not serve God with joy in plenty, they should serve their enemies in hunger, the punishment answering the sin.

themes: servedst not the LORD with joyfulness
for the abundance of all things
unthankful service
ingratitude in plenty

schools.summary: The servitude to enemies, the answer to ingratitude — 'therefore shalt thou serve thine enemies... in hunger, and in thirst, and in nakedness, and in want of all things: and he shall put a yoke of iron upon thy neck'; since they would not serve the LORD, so good a master who supplied them with all good things, they should serve cruel enemies in the utter lack of all things, the want the exact opposite of the abundance they ungratefully enjoyed (Gill); because they would not serve God, they became slaves to men, the refusal of the willing joyful service of God issuing in the hard compelled service of enemies (Clarke); 'a yoke of iron upon thy neck, until he have destroyed thee' — a servitude heavy, galling, and inescapable; no escaping service, the only choice whom we serve, God whose service is freedom or enemies whose service is bondage, those who cast off the easy yoke of God taking on the iron yoke of their foes; the service of God the truest liberty.

themes: serve thine enemies in hunger and want
a yoke of iron upon thy neck
want for plenty, slavery for service
the service of God is liberty

schools.summary: The nation from afar — 'the LORD shall bring a nation against thee from far, from the end of the earth, as swift as the eagle flieth; a nation whose tongue thou shalt not understand'; a distant, swift, and strange people, coming with the speed of the eagle upon its prey, speaking a tongue the people could not understand; the words in their fulness perhaps taking in both the Chaldean invasion under Nebuchadnezzar and the Roman under Titus, the prophecy reaching to more than one judgment; unknown and barbarous enemies coming with great impetuosity and violence (Calvin); the eagle's swiftness marking the suddenness and irresistibleness of the stroke, the foreign tongue the strangeness and terror of the invader.

debates.question: What nation is meant by 'a nation from far... as swift as the eagle... whose tongue thou shalt not understand'?

sides.position: the Romans — the eagle was the emblem on the Roman standards, the Roman eagle proverbial, and Latin a tongue the Jews knew not; Babylon was 'from far' but not 'from the end of the earth'
the Chaldeans/Babylonians — compare Jeremiah 5:15, 'a nation whose language thou knowest not,' spoken of Babylon
the prophecy may embrace both invasions, the Chaldean under Nebuchadnezzar and the Roman under Titus

themes: a nation from far, from the end of the earth
as swift as the eagle
tongue thou shalt not understand
Romans or Chaldeans

schools.summary: The merciless invader — 'a nation of fierce countenance, which shall not regard the person of the old, nor shew favour to the young'; 'strong of face,' a bold, hard, terrible people fearing none, aptly the old Romans, always fierce and unyielding, as the Chaldeans too were dreadful and terrible (Hab 1:7, Gill); the conqueror showing no respect to the aged whom common humanity reverences, nor pity to the young and helpless whom nature spares, the like word of the Chaldeans who 'had no compassion upon young man or maiden, old man or him that stooped for age' (2 Chr 36:17, Ellicott); the invader utterly without compassion, slaughtering the gray-headed and the children alike, the war waged with a savagery that knew no bounds; fitting both the Babylonian and the Roman destructions, in each the aged and young perishing without mercy; those who refused the mercy of God falling into the hands of the merciless.

themes: a nation of fierce countenance
not regard the old nor the young
merciless cruelty
the rod fierce as the sin was great

schools.summary: The invader consuming all — 'he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, and the fruit of thy land, until thou be destroyed... he shall not leave thee either corn, wine, or oil'; the enemy eating up the increase of their herds and flocks, larger and lesser, the calves and lambs and kids, and the fruit of the ground as well, all that the land and cattle yielded going to feed the conqueror (Gill); the enemy leaving them nothing, neither the staples of bread, wine, and oil, nor the young of their cattle and sheep, the land eaten down to bareness and the people brought to destruction by want; an invading army living off the land, consuming in its march all the substance of the people, so that famine followed the sword, the conqueror's consuming and the people's starving one; war and famine coming together as twin scourges of God's wrath; the disobedient eaten out of house and land, the enemy God's rod to consume them.

themes: eat the fruit of thy cattle and land
not leave thee corn, wine, or oil
the land stripped bare
war and famine together

schools.summary: The besieging of their strongholds — 'he shall besiege thee in all thy gates, until thy high and fenced walls come down, wherein thou trustedst'; in all their cities and walled towns which had gates and bars for defence, the enemy besetting every stronghold (Gill); the very walls they relied on thrown down, God overthrowing their false confidence, for the number and strength of their fortified towns had inspired a vain courage as if no enemy could persist to take them all (Calvin); Nebuchadnezzar first, who besieged and took Jerusalem (2 Kgs 25:1), and Titus next, the Roman, the enemy besetting them round and casting a trench about them — the very thing our Lord foretold of Jerusalem's siege (Luke 19:43, Clarke); the sieges of the last fenced cities by Nebuchadnezzar (Jer 34:7) and the Romans (Ellicott); the strongest defences unable to save a people whom God has given up, the walls they trusted falling that they might learn their trust was vain.

themes: besiege thee in all thy gates
thy high and fenced walls come down
wherein thou trustedst
trusted strongholds cannot save

schools.summary: The horror of siege-cannibalism — 'thou shalt eat the fruit of thine own body, the flesh of thy sons and of thy daughters... in the siege, and in the straitness'; nothing more shocking or unnatural, the famine of the siege driving the people to the most monstrous of crimes, the devouring of their own offspring, the besieged city cut off from all food reduced to such extremity (Gill); a portent detestable and more than tragical, that fathers and mothers should eat their own children, whom nature so strongly teaches them to love, the very bond of nature broken by the rage of famine (Calvin); confirmed to the letter in the siege of Samaria, where two women agreed to boil and eat their sons (2 Kgs 6:28-29), in the siege of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar, where the pitiful women sod their own children (Lam 4:10), and again in the Roman siege (Ellicott); no curse more terrible, nor prophecy more exactly fulfilled; sin fully ripened in judgment driving men below the beasts.

themes: eat the fruit of thine own body
the flesh of thy sons and daughters
in the siege and the straitness
famine breaks the bond of nature

schools.summary: The gentlest man made cruel by famine — 'the man that is tender among you, and very delicate, his eye shall be evil toward his brother, and toward the wife of his bosom... the remnant of his children'; the man bred softly and used to delicate living, naturally gentle and kind, not hard or rustic but of refined and tender nature (Gill, Keil); 'evil' meaning unkind, envious, and covetous — this tender man, in the extremity of the famine, grudging to share even the horrible food (the flesh of his own children) with his own brother, beloved wife, and remaining children, looking with a grudging eye on his dearest relations (Wesley); famine so unmanning the gentlest that he who by nature would have given his life for his family now grudges them a morsel of the most dreadful food, the tenderest affections turned to a fierce miserly cruelty; extreme suffering corrupting the best of natures; the curse striking not only the body but the heart, hardening love into cruelty.

themes: the man tender and very delicate
his eye shall be evil
toward his brother, wife, and children
famine turns love to cruelty

schools.summary: The hoarding of the unspeakable food — 'he will not give to any of them of the flesh of his children whom he shall eat: because he hath nothing left him in the siege'; the man driven to eat his own child yet, in his extremity, refusing to give any part of that unspeakable food even to his own brother, wife, or remaining children, his selfishness so total he would keep the horror to himself; the famine so utter, nothing else left in the besieged city, that even this most dreadful food was clung to and grudged (Keil); a complication of horrors — that they should eat their own children and then refuse to share even this, dreadfulness piled on dreadfulness (Ellicott); the famine not only driving men to the unnatural crime but stripping the last shred of natural affection, the father grudging his own family a part of his own murdered child, love, pity, and shame all consumed by hunger; the very bottom of the curse, the utter ruin of nature and of the family.

themes: will not give of the flesh of his children
nothing left him in the siege
horror piled on horror
the ruin of natural affection

schools.summary: The gentlest woman made cruel — 'the tender and delicate woman... her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter'; instanced because of her sex, naturally more pitiful, and especially one bred so genteelly and softly she would scarcely set her foot on the ground, the gentlest and most delicate of women (Gill); 'evil' meaning unmerciful, this tender mother in the famine designing even the destruction of her own husband and children for her food, grudging them and looking with a cruel eye (Wesley); literally and exactly fulfilled when Jerusalem was besieged by the Romans, in the case of Mary of Beth-ezob, a woman of noble family who, driven to distraction by famine, boiled and ate her own infant child, told with horrible minuteness by Josephus and after him by Eusebius (Clarke, Ellicott); the famine corrupting even a mother's love, the strongest of natural affections, that she should devour her own child; the prophecy, written near a thousand years before, fulfilled to the letter.

themes: the tender and delicate woman
her eye shall be evil
Mary of Beth-ezob, Josephus
a mother's love corrupted by famine

schools.summary: The secret eating of her own children — 'toward her young one... and toward her children which she shall bear: for she shall eat them for want of all things secretly in the siege'; 'her young one' the secundine or afterbirth, a thing loathsome even to look upon, which yet in the famine she should be driven to eat, and with it the newborn child wrapped in it, and her other children too (Gill, Clarke, Wesley); 'secretly in the siege' — done in secret, both from the shame of so dreadful a deed and from the fear that others, as starved, should snatch the food, the want so total that even this was clung to and hidden; the woman whose nature was all tenderness reduced to eat what is most loathsome and most dear, the famine breaking through every barrier of nature, decency, and love; the most terrible curse in all Scripture, the most exactly fulfilled in the sieges of Jerusalem; the witnesses dwelling on these things that the dreadfulness of forsaking God might be felt, the curse as great as the covenant was gracious.

themes: her young one, the afterbirth
eat them for want of all things
secretly in the siege
the uttermost horror of forsaking God

schools.summary: The condition and the fear of God's name — 'if thou wilt not observe to do all the words of this law... that thou mayest fear this glorious and fearful name, THE LORD THY GOD'; the words of the law written in the book of Deuteronomy, the curses coming upon the failure to observe and do them all (Gill); the keeping of the law to issue in and express the fear of God's great and dreadful name, the reverence of God the root of all obedience, the want of it the root of all the disobedience that brings the curse; 'this glorious and fearful name' — the name of the LORD, in which all his majesty and holiness are summed, glorious to be adored and fearful to be dreaded, to fear that name the beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10); even true believers, though willing, still hindered by the infirmity of the flesh, so God in mercy adds these fearful sanctions to keep even his own people in awe and spur them on lest they grow careless (Calvin); the fear of God the safeguard of obedience.

themes: if thou wilt not observe to do all the law
fear this glorious and fearful name
THE LORD THY GOD
the fear of God the root of obedience

schools.summary: The wonderful and lasting plagues — 'the LORD will make thy plagues wonderful, and the plagues of thy seed, even great plagues, and of long continuance, and sore sicknesses, and of long continuance'; 'wonderful' as visible, remarkable, and distinguishable, astonishing to all who saw them, the judgments no ordinary calamities but such as marked the hand of God, extraordinary in kind and degree (Gill); the strokes great in severity and long in endurance, not passing quickly but cleaving to the people, reaching to their seed after them, the children sharing the plagues of the fathers; a great affliction that swiftly passes may be borne, but one both great and lasting, wearing on without relief, among the heaviest of strokes, God threatening not a momentary blow but an enduring scourge; God had promised in obedience to take all sickness from them (Ex 15:26), in disobedience to make their plagues wonderful and long; the wonder of the plagues a testimony to the wonder of the sin.

themes: make thy plagues wonderful
great plagues of long continuance
sore sicknesses
extraordinary and enduring judgment

schools.summary: The diseases of Egypt brought back — 'he will bring upon thee all the diseases of Egypt, which thou wast afraid of; and they shall cleave unto thee'; either the diseases God brought on the Egyptians in judgment (the plagues) or the diseases peculiar to Egypt, the maladies Israel had seen and feared (Gill); the exact reversal of the promise of Ex 15:26, where God said 'I will put none of these diseases of Egypt upon thee... for I am the LORD that healeth thee,' the very diseases God pledged in obedience to keep from them brought upon them in disobedience (Ellicott); 'they shall cleave unto thee' — the diseases not passing but fastening and staying, an abiding affliction, the curse holding fast where it lighted; the people afflicted with the very plagues they had seen fall on their oppressors and been thankful to escape; the mercies God gives in obedience reversed in rebellion, to forsake the LORD who heals being to fall back under the diseases he removed.

themes: all the diseases of Egypt
which thou wast afraid of
they shall cleave unto thee
the healing reversed to plague

schools.summary: The unnamed plagues — 'also every sickness, and every plague, which is not written in the book of this law, them will the LORD bring upon thee, until thou be destroyed'; whatever sickness or disease could be thought of or named, even those not mentioned or threatened in the law, God would bring, the written curses not the whole but a sample, God having in store more than the book could contain (Gill); the word 'plague' (makkah, a smiting) having a wide meaning, comprehending all the curses, the sickness named as a part and the plague taking in the whole (Calvin); in the face of such a threat — every conceivable and inconceivable plague brought by God himself — well might the Apostle write 'It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God' (Heb 10:31, Ellicott); the people unable by escaping the named plagues to escape the wrath, God having ways of judgment beyond their reckoning; the resources of God's wrath as inexhaustible as the riches of his mercy.

themes: every sickness not written in the book
God will bring upon thee
until thou be destroyed
a fearful thing to fall into God's hands

schools.summary: The people reduced to a handful — 'ye shall be left few in number, whereas ye were as the stars of heaven for multitude; because thou wouldest not obey the voice of the LORD thy God'; very few left in Judea by Nebuchadnezzar's general when Jerusalem was taken, those of the poorer sort, left for vinedressers and husbandmen (Gill), Israel almost annihilated by the plagues and the sword (Keil); since it had been promised to Abraham that his seed should be as the stars of heaven (Gen 15:5), it was a signal and dreadful token of God's wrath that his posterity should be reduced to a mere handful, the very promise of increase reversed (Calvin); all this coming of their disobedience, the dwindling of the nation the just fruit of refusing to hear God; the blessings God gives, even those founded on his promise to the fathers, enjoyed only in the way of obedience; God able as easily to diminish as to multiply, the same hand that made them as the stars leaving them a handful.

themes: left few in number
as the stars of heaven for multitude
because thou wouldest not obey
the promise of increase reversed

schools.summary: God rejoicing in judgment as in mercy — 'as the LORD rejoiced over you to do you good... so the LORD will rejoice over you to destroy you, and to bring you to nought'; the same God who had taken delight in doing them good, multiplying and blessing them, taking a kind of pleasure in their ruin when they forsook him, the contrast measuring the change; the wonderful and inestimable love of God set forth, in that he had rejoiced in heaping blessings, making their depravity all the more base that they should so requite him (Calvin); the careful distinction — God does not delight in the death of a sinner in itself (Ezek 33:11), but he delights in glorifying his justice, and so, when his patience is despised and mercy refused, takes a holy satisfaction in executing the judgment that vindicates his righteousness (Wesley); the God slow to anger and rich in mercy yet magnifying his justice in the destruction of the impenitent; to presume on God's love being to forget the same God is just.

themes: as the LORD rejoiced to do you good
so he will rejoice to destroy you
his justice glorified
mercy refused turns to judgment

schools.summary: The scattering among all nations — 'the LORD shall scatter thee among all people, from the one end of the earth even unto the other; and there thou shalt serve other gods... even wood and stone'; their present dispersion, more or fewer, in all parts of the world, east, west, north, and south, how literally fulfilled, the Jews scattered over every nation under heaven, fulfilled to the letter in this last and lasting dispersion under the Romans (Gill, Clarke, Ellicott); banishment far more painful to Israel than to other nations, affection for one's country natural to all, and theirs the land of promise, the holy land, from which to be cast out a peculiar grief (Calvin); the spiritual peril — in their dispersion among idolatrous nations drawn to serve false gods, though Israel not known to fall into actual idolatry in dispersion save in Egypt (Jer 44:8, Ellicott); the Jew in every land a standing wonder, the prophecy written in their very condition; the people who would not serve God in their own land scattered among the nations.

themes: scatter thee among all people
from one end of the earth to the other
serve other gods, wood and stone
the Jew in every land

schools.summary: The restless terror of the dispersed — 'among these nations shalt thou find no ease, neither shall the sole of thy foot have rest: but the LORD shall give thee there a trembling heart, and failing of eyes, and sorrow of mind'; no quiet settlement nor certain dwelling, driven from place to place by cruel edicts, heavy fines, and persecutions, tossed about, sold from person to person, Cain-like wandering without a resting-place (Gill, Wesley); the trembling heart their state of continual insecurity, everywhere proscribed and uncertain of toleration, the failing of eyes the looking for a relief that never surely came, the sorrow of mind the abiding grief of so unsettled a life (Clarke); the repeated persecutions of the Jews in the ages of dispersion — expulsions, massacres, proscriptions — among the most fearful and wonderful phenomena of history, the picture this verse drew near a thousand years before (Ellicott); to forsake God, the rest of the soul, being to find no rest anywhere.

themes: no ease, nor rest for the sole of thy foot
a trembling heart, failing eyes
sorrow of mind
no rest for those who forsake God

schools.summary: The constant fear of death — 'thy life shall hang in doubt before thee; and thou shalt fear day and night, and shalt have none assurance of thy life'; their life like some valued object hung up before their eyes, always in danger of being torn down and snatched away, held by a thread, ever in peril (Keil); not knowing from one moment to the next whether their life would be spared by the enemy, Rashi's gloss 'perhaps I shall die today by the sword that cometh upon me' (Gill, Ellicott); the terror springing both from their outward state, in the hands of enemies with power and will to destroy, and their inward state, the terrors of their own mind and the guilt of conscience making them their own tormentors (Wesley); to have no assurance of one's very life, to fear death every hour, a misery worse than death itself; the people who would not trust God for their life losing all assurance, the fear of death from which God's people are freed (Heb 2:15) holding them in lifelong bondage.

themes: thy life shall hang in doubt
fear day and night
no assurance of thy life
the fear of death in lifelong bondage

schools.summary: The unrelieved misery of fear — 'in the morning thou shalt say, Would God it were even! and at even thou shalt say, Would God it were morning! for the fear of thine heart... and for the sight of thine eyes'; in the morning wishing the day already over, fearing their life taken or some fresh calamity before night, and at evening wishing it were morning, the terrors of the night worse than the day, no hour bringing rest but each dreaded and longed to be past (Gill); the inward dread of their heart and the outward horrors their eyes beheld together making every hour a torment; the trouble ever increasing, so that yesterday's evening was longed for this morning, but today's trouble more terrible still, every hour adding to the curse, no point of rest but a worsening misery (Ellicott, the Talmud); to find no ease at any hour, to long always for the hour to pass and dread the next, the very picture of a life forsaken of all comfort; those who forsook the God of all comfort left without comfort day or night.

themes: would God it were even! would God it were morning!
for the fear of thine heart
the sight of thine eyes
no ease at any hour

schools.summary: The final return to bondage — 'the LORD shall bring thee into Egypt again with ships... and there ye shall be sold unto your enemies for bondmen and bondwomen, and no man shall buy you'; the people carried back to Egypt, the house of their old bondage, strictly and literally; the pointed contrast in 'with ships' — whereas at the Exodus they went out by land through the divided sea in triumph, now brought back over the sea in ships as captives, the way of their redemption reversed (Gill, Calvin, Wesley); sold into slavery, the very thing from which God had redeemed their fathers, the redeemed people made slaves again; 'no man shall buy you' — exposed for sale, but the supply of captives so great and they so despised that none would even buy them, the lowest depth of contempt (Clarke); Josephus recording this done with multitudes of the Jews by Titus after the fall of Jerusalem, carried into Egypt in ships and sold, so cheap and many that none would buy (Ellicott); the chapter ending in this uttermost curse, the redemption undone, pointing beyond itself to the curse of the law from which Christ alone redeems (Gal 3:13).

themes: bring thee into Egypt again with ships
sold for bondmen and bondwomen
no man shall buy you
the redemption utterly reversed
